comments:
Summary and contributions
The paper presents a finitary reformulation of the standard coherence criterion for lower previsions when these are essentially defined on a finite set of gambles over a finite possibility space. The author shows how to generate a finite (though not always irredundant) H-representation of the convex polytope of coherent lower previsions, and how to obtain its V-representation (extreme coherent lower previsions) by vertex enumeration. The work draws heavily on polytope theory (Minkowski–Weyl, Fourier–Motzkin elimination, Carathéodory) and on prior foundational work in imprecise probabilities (Walley, Quaeghebeur & De Cooman). The paper includes several illustrative computational experiments and combinatorial analyses for various classes of gamble sets.

Strengths
- The paper addresses a clear theoretical gap: giving a most-general finitary characterization of coherent lower previsions on finite gamble sets. The connection to H- and V-representations of polytopes is natural and well exploited.
- Definitions are carefully developed in a stepwise fashion, showing how one can systematically reduce the infinite family of constraints to a finite generating family (culminating in Definition 9).
- Theoretical lemmas (elimination of linear dependence, preservation of linear independence) are appropriate and appear to knit the argument together.
- The paper provides computational illustrations, giving an empirical feel for the combinatorial growth of constraints and vertices in several meaningful classes of examples.

Weaknesses and points for improvement
- Practical scalability: the author acknowledges that the approach is computationally demanding in realistic settings. More explicit discussion and quantitative analysis of complexity (time, memory) for the implemented experiments would be valuable. For example, run-times and memory footprints for the larger experiments would help readers assess feasibility.
- Redundancy and irredundant generation: while Definition 9 is finitary, the generated constraint sets may still contain many redundant constraints. The paper suggests redundancy removal is advisable but does not detail principled ways to avoid generating large numbers of redundant constraints up-front. A discussion of heuristics or theoretical conditions that yield irredundant families (or at least reduce redundancy) would strengthen the practical contribution.
- Comparison with alternative computational approaches: the paper references Fourier–Motzkin elimination and public polyhedral software, but lacks a clear comparison with alternative methods for checking coherence or constructing credal sets (e.g., direct linear programming checks, other elimination/vertex-enumeration pipelines). A brief comparative evaluation or discussion would help place the contribution in context.
- Proofs and reproducibility: the main text repeatedly refers to (Proofs), (Implementation), (Output) etc., but these appear to be external supplements. The paper would be stronger if key proof sketches were included in the main body or in a clear appendix, and if links or explicit references to the implementation and datasets were provided for reproducibility. Also, it is not always clear which parts are proven versus asserted; clearer pointers or included proofs would improve rigor and reader confidence.
- Presentation and notation: the exposition is generally clear, but dense in places. Some definitions introduce multiple technical restrictions in rapid succession (normalizations, adding singleton indicators, linear independence constraints). A compact algorithmic summary (pseudocode) that describes the full pipeline (constraint generation, redundancy removal, Fourier–Motzkin projection, vertex enumeration) would help readers understand practical implementation steps.

Soundness and correctness
Within the provided text, the logical flow is plausible and consistent with standard results from polytope theory and the theory of lower previsions. The lemmas and reductions appear to follow established techniques (normalization, Carathéodory reductions, projection). Since full proofs are stated as provided elsewhere in the manuscript's supplements, I rate soundness as good but would prefer inclusion of or specific references to the proofs for independent checking.

Suggestions for the author
- Include runtime and memory usage statistics for the computational experiments and specify the software packages (versions) and hardware used.
- Add pseudocode or a flowchart summarizing the full computational pipeline, and outline heuristics for handling redundancy in constraint generation.
- Provide (or link to) the implementation and output files referenced in the text to facilitate reproducibility.
- Expand the discussion comparing this finitary approach to alternative computational/optimization based methods for checking coherence, and discuss when each approach is preferable.
- If possible, investigate or at least speculate on approaches to produce irredundant generating constraint sets (structural theorems, symmetry exploitation, or problem-dependent pruning strategies).

Overall recommendation
This paper makes a solid theoretical contribution to the foundations of imprecise probability by giving a general finitary characterization of coherent lower previsions on finite gamble sets and demonstrating how to compute extreme elements via polytope methods. Its practical applicability is currently limited by computational cost, but the work opens avenues for future research into more efficient irredundant criteria and specialized algorithms. I recommend the paper for dissemination (high recommendation).
